data.title: Travere Therapeutics: Still Undervalued Post FSGS Approval
data.description:
Travere Therapeutics overview

In my previous article titled "Travere Therapeutics: Filspari's FSGS Approval Chance Is Higher Than You Think", I explained why I believe Filspari will be approved for FSGS. Recently that approval came, and TVTX stock shot up on the news. In this article, I will dive into the details of that approval and the commercial prospects. Overall, I think TVTX is still undervalued based on projected revenue and therefore is a Buy at these levels.

Filspari's approval for FSGS

On April 13, 2026, the FDA granted full approval to Filspari (Sparsentan) for the treatment of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), making it the first and only medication specifically approved for this rare kidney disease. Indicated for both adults and pediatric patients aged 8 years and older who do not have nephrotic syndrome. The drug works by targeting dual pathways to reduce proteinuria and slow kidney damage. This landmark decision was supported by the Phase 3 DUPLEX study, which demonstrated a significant 46% reduction in proteinuria over 108 weeks compared to standard-of-care irbesartan. Despite an earlier regulatory delay and a primary endpoint miss regarding long-term kidney function in the overall study population, the FDA's approval underscores the clinical importance of proteinuria reduction in managing FSGS and provides a critical new option for a patient population that previously relied on off-label therapies.

The key thing to focus here is although this is a full approval, it does come with a restrictor which says the drug can only be used for patients who do not have nephrotic syndrome (NS). This is based on the phase 3 DUPLEX study sub-population analysis, where it showed patients without NS benefited more consistently from Filspari treatment.

Nephrotic syndrome exclusion: Not a huge concern

Nephrotic syndrome is, and this is from the CMO of TVTX:"Nephrotic syndrome is a clinical diagnosis, which commonly refers to meeting all 3 of the following criteria: high levels of proteinuria greater than 3.5 grams per day, low serum albumin of less than 3 grams per deciliter and the presence of edema."

This is not a huge concern, as the CMO of TVTX estimated only 20% of the FSGS patients enrolled in DUPLEX had active NS, and they come in and out of it, and once they are no longer in NS, they can be treated with Filspari. In other words, once the FSGS patient no longer meets any of the 3 NS criteria, they become eligible for Filspari treatments. So the actual % of FSGS patients who are not eligible for Filspari treatments is likely even less than 20% of the total 30,000 patient population.

Filspari in FSGS: Constructive on the launch

I am bullish on the commercial launch of Filspari in FSGS for two key reasons. First, Filspari is the only FDA-approved drug for this indication. Second, there is significant "cross-selling" potential with the IgAN indication. TVTX’s successful IgAN launch allows them to leverage existing commercial infrastructure—including prescribers, payers, and CMC production—for FSGS. This represents a unique cross-selling opportunity because the two indications share so many similarities, which I believe should reduce commercialization costs while increasing market penetration.

There's also a significant unmet need in FSGS treatment, as roughly 30% to 50% of patients with primary FSGS potentially progress to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) and need a kidney transplant, and even then up to 30-40% relapse after a kidney transplant (see below figure).

TVTX stock valuation with FSGS approval

The CEO of TVTX mentioned 3B peak sales now with FSGS full approval:

"As we look out, we see the potential for peak year sales over $3 billion when we look at the combination of these 2 indications. And our job right now, as Peter mentioned, is to make sure that we're out there very quickly because patients deserve it and to be able to educate physicians on this label and the opportunity to do better in FSGS."

That is not a far stretch, but even if we use a more bearish projection of $2B peak sales for both indications combined and a PS ratio of 3, we still reach a valuation of $6B. As of today, TVTX is valued at $3.72B, indicating that the company's market cap should rise over time if they execute well.

Another point to note is that TVTX can be very profitable in just a year or so. According to Seeking Alpha data, the consensus EPS could be nearly $4.6 as early as next year, with a forward PE of 8.84. Considering the sector-wide median forward PE of ~18, and again this indicates that TVTX is still significantly undervalued.

From a market penetration perspective, looking at Filspari in IgAN in 2025, total revenue in 2025 was $322M, the per-patient per year listing price was $120k, and Gross-to-Net (GTN) reduction was about 20%. As such, the total number of IgAN patients treated in 2025 was about:

$322M/($120k X 80%) = $3,354

Since there are a total of 70,000 IgAN patients treatable with Filspari, this indicates only ~5% market penetration as of 2025 (in line with management's estimate of current penetration being <10%). If we assume peak market penetration of 20%, and GTN deteriorates to a 28% reduction due to expanding access, peak sales for Filspari in IgAN alone could be:

70,000 X 20% X $120k X 72% = $1.2B

For FSGS revenue, because per patient the dose of Filspari is 800-mg which doubles that of IgAN, per patient revenue is much higher, say 50% more at $180k per patient per year. Since there is much less competition in FSGS than IgAN, we will assume peak market penetration of 25% (lower due to the NS exclusion requirement), with GTN reduction of 25%. This gives a peak revenue of:

30,000 X 25% X $180k X 75% = 1B

As such, we reach a total peak revenue of $2.2B, not so far from the CEO's $3B prediction. This goes to show that if the commercial execution goes well, there is significant upside potential for TVTX.

Risks

The main risks are that the commercial launch of Filspari in FSGS and the continued expansion in IgAN fail to impress. IgAN is becoming a crowded field with major players like Novartis and Vertex entering the market. However, FSGS is currently a significant "white space," with Filspari serving as the only FDA-approved drug. I imagine that in the coming quarters, investors will weigh FSGS PSF numbers and pricing heavily to assess the overall commercial launch in this key indication. Given the synergies with the existing IgAN commercial infrastructure, I believe the launch will be both rapid and expansive.

The other risk is patent expiration or LOE. Filspari (sparsentan) holds key patent protections and FDA exclusivities, including a major patent (US9993461) expiring March 29, 2030, and an IgAN indication exclusivity running through February 17, 2030. TVYX management expects Filspari to have LOE until at least 2033 due to expected orphan drug exclusivity and is working to expand it further. 7 years is still a long time to make the best of it, and I would argue this potentially makes TVTX a good buyout target given the recent increased interest in kidney diseases by big pharma.

Final remarks

Post-FSGS approval, TVTX looks undervalued based on both peak sales and forward PE. If the company is able to launch Filspari in FSGS successfully in the upcoming quarters, a significant re-rating of its stock price is likely. Using a peak sale of $2.2B and PS of 3, TVTX's fair valuation should be between $5-$6.6B today, and that is not counting any pipeline progress. Overall, I rate TVTX a BUY.

data.title: AMD Stock: My $300 Target Is Close - Here's What Comes Next
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares have been getting a major boost in recent weeks as AI spending takes center stage, reported shortages are increasing CPU prices, and the company's data center business continues to boom. I remain bullish on AMD's prospects going into the next earnings report on May 5th and into the second half of 2026.

In my last piece on AMD back in February, titled "$300 Appears Imminent; Why I'm Buying More," I highlighted the company's strong operating results in the data center and argued valuation multiple convergence would lift the stock to all-time highs in short order. Less than two months since that call, shares are already up 40% and are closing in on my target. That article can be read here.

So what caused the sudden momentum shift? There are probably a few factors at play: the easing of tensions in the Iran war led equities to all-time highs, CPUs appear to be facing a supply shortage, and AI infrastructure stocks have been surging in general as massive capex comes to fruition. Whatever the exact catalysts, I think this has been a long time coming— AMD has been executing on its roadmap and delivering on its promises to narrow the gap with the top of the compute food chain. It was only a matter of time, in my opinion, before the stock price began to reflect that reality.

In recent weeks, AMD has undeniably been getting a boost, along with Intel (INTC), from reports that the AI boom is causing a shortage in processors, which could lead to a 15% increase in price for general purpose server CPUs in 2026. If this sounds familiar, that might be because the exact same thing is playing out in the memory market as prices are soaring in what many are dubbing the "RAMpocalypse." In response, the stock of DRAM and NAND manufacturer Micron (MU) is up 570% over the last 12 months. Is this a glimpse into AMD's future?

On the one hand, a Digitimes report recently claimed that the CPU shortage is "more acute" than the memory shortage because memory is still available in limited quantities but CPUs are sold out for months. On the other hand, while you might initially think this implies that AMD and Intel are about to see a Micron-like boom, there is one, vital reason why this is highly unlikely to be the case: volume.

CPU volumes are substantially lower than DRAM and NAND by orders of magnitude, meaning a shortage in the former translates into higher margins and modestly higher revenue while a shortage in the latter translates into earning multiple years worth of net income in a single quarter. So the shortage you've been hearing about in the news likely doesn't mean that AMD and INTC are going to double in the next month, but it does mean that CPUs are supply constrained and in high demand, which will keep prices and margins high.

And, similar to what we've seen in the memory market, the shortage is concentrated in the high-end data center market, but since production lines are limited, processor manufacturers will shift away from low-end markets towards higher margin customers and the price increases and shortages will ripple down the supply chain. For AMD, this could have the effect of boosting margins throughout all of its CPU segments, which is exactly what happened to Micron:

Even low-margin divisions like Automotive & Embedded and Mobile & Client saw margins shoot higher and, if a CPU supply shortage persists, I think we might see a similar effect on AMD's lower margin segments like Embedded and Gaming and also, of course, on its higher margin Client and Data Center segments.

Part of this shortage can be attributed to Intel's slower-than-expected rollout of its next-generation 18A process node, which the company has promised will challenge TSMC's (TSM) dominance in premium silicon. I have long been bullish about this node, and though yields have not yet improved enough for true high-volume manufacturing, Intel has been able to produce its own processors using the technology even if yields are not yet high enough to procure and manufacture chips for external customers.

The current scarcity environment has proven favorable to AMD, which contracts its processors through TSMC — a company that is most assuredly not having yield issues. Intel is sending out Clearwater Forest server CPUs built on 18A to a few data center customers and expects a ramp in 2H2026, but delays have plagued the company's newest process nodes for over a decade so whether that comes to pass or whether the market remains in a deep supply shortage remains to be seen. Personally, I'm betting we end up getting both of those scenarios.

This all also happens to be well timed with the launch of AMD's latest line of server CPUs, codenamed Venice, which is slated for a 2H2026 release date. Venice features the Zen 6 architecture and will be produced on TSMC's bleeding-edge N2P node, which claims a nearly 20% performance boost with a 36% reduction in power over the N3E process used to manufacture Turin Dense. With TSMC at its back, AMD has stormed back in the market share race with Intel (data is from Mercury Research):

Previously languishing in the single-digits a decade ago, AMD now commands a unit share and revenue share of 36.4% and 42.6%, respectively, in the desktop CPU market and 28.8% and 41.3% unit and revenue share in server CPUs. And the trend has been relentless.

While unit share is, of course, important, revenue share is the true measure of sales that translate to net income, and on that front, AMD is up 1460 bps YoY in desktop and 490 bps in server as a process node advantage, courtesy of TSMC, has given EPYC chips a leg-up in efficiency and power over Intel's offerings. This gap is especially pronounced in the data center where performance per watt is essential and hyperscalers can ill afford to cheap out. This means that, until 18A achieves the requisite yields to actually make a dent in industry demand, AMD will remain far-and-away the leader in premium CPUs.

And beyond the current favorable pricing environment and AMD's CPU leadership, the company has also continued to push the envelope in the AI accelerator market against the likes of Nvidia (NVDA). While Nvidia is still the king of training, AMD's MI455X, expected to release in the second half of 2026, is an inference powerhouse that delivers raw performance on par with Nvidia's Blackwell and boasts a massive 432 GB of HBM4 memory, which dwarfs Blackwell's 192 GB / 288 GB (B200 vs. B300) and significantly outpaces next-generation Vera Rubin's 288 GB. There are certainly other metrics that matter in this fight where Nvidia still has an edge, such as bandwidth and speed, but AMD has undoubtedly closed the gap and has cemented itself as a viable alternative in a sector that needs all the capacity it can get.

Lastly, AMD will also be launching its Helios rack-scale servers in tandem with the MI455X, which will allow it to compete with Nvidia on providing a completely vertically-integrated AI stack to data center operators. These racks are key to AMD's ability to challenge Nvidia for hyperscaler business, which has already been paying dividends.

Investor Takeaway

I think it's fair to say that AMD is firing on all cylinders right now across all of its product segments; with CPUs in short supply, pricing is ticking up, which could lead to record margins in the coming quarters. Shares are already rising quite sharply towards my $300 milestone, but I still think there is more room to run in the short-term on the back of the ongoing supply shortage and the AI sector's current major momentum upswing. Market volatility has remained elevated and I think the sector is still quite vulnerable to large swings so those with a low risk tolerance might want to be cautious.

In the long-term, I continue to see AMD as an undervalued opportunity at the forefront of powerful secular trends. The company is an innovator that constantly matches and leapfrogs well-funded opposition using a strong culture of engineering excellence and strategic thinking. As such, I am reiterating a Strong Buy on AMD and will be adding on pullbacks on the way up.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: Avis Budget: Shorts Are Running Out Of Options
data.description:
Avis Budget (CAR) closed at $608 on April 20th up about 500% in five weeks.

I am writing this because what is happening with this stock right now is, in my opinion, the most fascinating event in the equity market since the Volkswagen short squeeze in 2008. I think many people watching it do not fully understand the mechanics behind it.

If you have been following this story you probably already know the basics. A heavily shorted rental car company got squeezed and the stock went parabolic. But the story is not that simple.

Two hedge funds accumulated over 100% of the Avis economic interest through a combination of stock and swaps, and then one of them pulled the trigger on an options expiration date that forced the other side to deliver shares that did not exist in the open market. In this piece I will walk you through exactly how that happened, step by step because I think the mechanics are important and I have not seen many people explain them properly.

Now if you are wondering how it is even possible for two funds to own more than 100% of a company. So, when a short seller borrows a share and sells it, the buyer on the other side now also owns that same share. So the same share gets counted twice, once by the original lender who still shows it on their books, and once by the new buyer. That is how total ownership across all holders can exceed 100% of shares available.

On top of that, total return swaps give you the economic exposure of owning shares without actually holding them. So when SRS and Pentwater combined their direct stock holdings with their swap positions their total economic interest went above 100% of the company. They do not own more shares than exist. But economically they control more than 100% of the upside and downside.

What Does Avis Actually Do

Before we get into the squeeze mechanics, let me quickly cover the business itself, because understanding why shorts were so aggressively shorting is half the story.

Avis Budget Group owns the Avis, Budget, Zipcar, Payless and Apex rental car brands. They run a fleet of about 684k vehicles. They report earnings in two segments, the Americas, which makes up about 76% of revenue, roughly $8.9 billion in 2025 and International, which covers EMEA and Asia Pacific at around 24%.

Now here is the part that mattered for the shorts. Their FY25 was ugly. Revenue came in at $11.65 billion, down about 1% y/y. They posted a net loss of $995 million. Diluted EPS was negative $25.26. And adjusted EBITDA of $748 million missed prior guidance of roughly $900 million, entirely on the Americas side.

The Q4 FY25 report, which dropped on February 18 was especially bad. They reported an $856 million quarterly loss that included a $518 million writedown on the US EV rental fleet. If that sounds familiar, it is basically the same mistake Hertz made with Tesla in 2024.

On top of that, Americas segment revenue per day was down 4%, and per-unit fleet depreciation went to $338 versus prior guidance below $300. By mid-March 2026 CAR was the most heavily shorted stock in the Russell 1000. And if you looked at the fundamentals alone it was easy to see why.

S3 Partners said that 49% of the available float sold short by mid-March and went to 54% by end of the month. That was about 9 million shares short against a publicly tradeable float that was arguably smaller than the short position itself.

Due to the smaller float the days to cover reached 7.3 and the borrow utilization hit 89%. As a result Cost to borrow went from sub-1% in early February into the 3% to 4.25% range.

The short case looked ironclad. Their total debt went to $28 billion, roughly $19 billion of non-recourse fleet ABS. And only about cash $524 million in cash.

Used car prices were softening which threatened the per-unit fleet depreciation math even further. The Q4 EV impairment confirmed that Avis had basically repeated Hertz's (HTZ) Tesla mistake on a big scale. Plus autonomous vehicles capped the potential long term demand.

On paper, this was a good short thesis. A dying business model, drowning in debt and losing nearly a billion dollars a year. In my opinion the shorts were right about the business but they were catastrophically wrong about the float.

Where Shorts Were Wrong

Avis has only 35.26 million shares outstanding. Which is a very small share count for a company this visible. And of those 35.26 million shares a huge amount were already locked up before the squeeze even started.

SRS Investment Management had held a position in Avis since 2018. By the end of last year SRS controlled about 49% of the economic interest. In September of last year Avis had even amended its cooperation agreement to lift SRS's voting cap from 35% to 45%.

So right there nearly half the company was locked up by a single fund. And these shares were not going to be sold. Then you add roughly 10% held in passive index vehicles like Vanguard or other ETFs.

So before the squeeze even started, somewhere around 59% of shares outstanding were basically off the table. The actual tradeable float was maybe 14 to 15 million shares. And against that, 9 million shares were sold short. More than 60% of the free float was short. And then a second hedge fund showed up and took most of what was left.

Pentwater Lit The Fuse

Pentwater Capital Management had been accumulating its Avis stake through 2025 and disclosed a 12.3% position of 4.33 million shares on February 13 via a Schedule 13G/A filing.

But between February 20 and 24 just two days after the stock tanked on the $856 million quarterly loss, Pentwater bought 425k more shares at an average price of around $94.26.

They sold puts expiring March 20, at strikes of $110, $120, $125 and $130. And they bought September 18 expiry calls at $80 and $85 strikes.

If this structure reminds you of something, it should. This is similar to what Porsche did to Volkswagen in 2008. Physically settled options that force share delivery, plus cash-settled swaps whose delta has to be hedged by dealers in the open market.

Every time the stock goes up, the dealer has to buy more stock to stay hedged. It is a feedback loop kind of situation. In my opinion the March 20 expiry started this whole cascade. On the monthly options expiration, Pentwater exercised all of the in-the-money puts. This forced the other side to deliver the shares in huge blocks, including a single 1,236,300-share tranche at $110, plus additional blocks at the $120, $125 and $130 strikes.

This resulted in borrow evaporation that pulled millions of shares into holdings that are not available for lending. The cost to borrow went to about 3.88%, which is roughly thirteen times the general collateral rate. If you are short and the lender recalls them or the broker cannot locate replacements to cover the borrow, you face a forced "buy-in".

This also created a gamma feedback loop. Dealers who were short Pentwater's September $80 and $85 calls had to buy stock as delta went up. At the same time delta hedges on the synthetic long swaps added more buying pressure with every leg up. This created the gamma squeeze loop where price going up forces more buying, which forces the stock to move up and so on.

By the end of March Pentwater's reported stake had reached about 22% or 7.8 million shares. Now combine that with SRS's 49% and the roughly 10% in passive index vehicles. Roughly 82% of shares outstanding were locked up by holders who were not selling. Against 9 million shares that had to be delivered to close short positions. There were more shares sold short than there were shares available to buy.

A Macro Tailwind Made It Worse

And then something else happened that nobody expected. A February federal funding lapse had left TSA screeners unpaid, which resulted in hours of security lines at Atlanta, Chicago and other major hubs. This pushed travelers toward road trips instead of flying, which is obviously bullish for rental car companies.

Hertz rallied about 65% on the same tailwind. But Avis rallied like ten times as much. This macro catalyst gave the squeeze a fundamental story to attach to, which pulled in momentum traders who might have otherwise stayed away.

Why It Is Not Over Yet

The squeeze is still active as of now and there are structural reasons why it has not unwound yet. The short interest is still very high and a huge portion of the short shares are yet to be covered.

Because SRS is an Avis insider. They are subject to insider trading restrictions and cannot sell until Q1 FY26 earnings are released, which are expected around May 6th. Their position is locked until then.

Also Pentwater is subject to the Section 16 short swing profit rule. This rule is basically freezing their position until roughly late September 2026. They bought shares in February and March, so any sale within six months would require them to return the profits to the company. The two biggest holders basically cannot sell, even if they wanted to.

If You Are Long, Walk Away

If you even bought this stock like 48 hours ago you are sitting on huge gains. Right now, CAR is not trading on anything that has to do with the actual business. It is trading on pure short squeeze mechanics and nothing else. I would say take the gains and run.

An analyst on Seeking Alpha, Gytis Zizys, wrote a really good piece about the current fundamentals of the stock. I would suggest reading that to get the complete picture of what you are actually owning at $600 per share. Every single short squeeze in history ends the same way. The stock comes back to reality. AMC, Bed Bath & Beyond and the 2021 CAR squeeze itself, all of them.

Bottom Line

Right now the stock is trading on float mechanics, not fundamentals. And the float mechanics say that more shares are sold short than are available to buy. This is not a stock I would touch on either side. At $600 plus, CAR is neither a sensible long nor a survivable short. But in my opinion, it is the most important market structure case study since the 2008 Volkswagen short squeeze.

data.title: ASML: Why I'm Not Buying Here
data.description:
Brief Overview

ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) is a Dutch (ASML (AMS)) multinational corporation famous for its criticality in the AI infrastructure buildout. It is a leading manufacturer of photolithography machines used in the manufacturing of semiconductor chips at semiconductor fabs. It holds a dominant position in the supply of Extreme Ultra-Violet [EUV] lithography machines.

ASML stock has nearly doubled in the past 12 months in a bull run that began after President Trump eased his stance on tariffs in the middle of April last year. The stock is also up nearly 39% YTD, making it the 13th-best performer within the Nasdaq 100.

In this article, I will dive into the company’s Q1 earnings, guidance for Q2, and valuations to understand why the stock is not quite ripe for fresh buying or first-time buyers but still worth holding in your portfolio.

Before I dive into the article, I would like to inform readers that the conversion rate I’m using is roughly $1.176 = €1, which is the spot rate as of the time of writing.

Upside Triggers:

Q1 Earnings: Guidance Upgraded and Operating Leverage

ASML reported its Q1 earnings in the middle of last week. In Q1, the company reported a revenue of 8.8 billion euros ($10.4 billion) against expectations of 8.5 billion euros ($10.01 billion). The company earned a Net Income of €2.756 billion ($3.24 billion), beating expectations of €2.5 billion ($2.94 billion). Q1 revenue was at the higher end of the company’s previously stated guidance of 8.2 billion euros to 8.9 billion euros.

Revenue grew 13% YoY, and gross margin came in at 53%, at the higher end of the company’s stated guidance, but 100 bps lower compared to the same quarter last year. Lower down the income statement, operating margins improved 60 bps YoY and net income margins improved 40 bps YoY. Profits from equity method investments more than tripled YoY from 33 million euros to 104.6 million euros. As a result, net income grew 17% YoY from €2.355 billion to €2.757 billion.

For the quarter ahead, the company expects to generate a revenue of 8.4 to 9 billion euros, reflecting 13% growth YoY at the midpoint of the guided range. For the full year 2026, the company expects to generate revenue between €36 billion and €40 billion, which is a significant upgrade from its previous guidance of €34 billion and €39 billion. At the midpoint of the latest guidance, the company expects 16% revenue growth for the full year, suggesting that H2 revenue growth should be at 19-20% YoY, stronger than H1 at 13%.

While this growth may be possible due to the strong demand for AI semiconductor products, with the first two quarters at 13%, I feel the conviction is missing to some degree, especially in the absence of orders/bookings data and net system sales growing slower than base management/service sales and, by extension, the overall sales growth.

To end on a positive note, what's helping ASML grow its profits faster than its sales growth is operating leverage. The company is able to achieve this by keeping control over R&D and SG&A expenses, which grew slower than total revenues in Q1 2026. As per the guidance for Q2 2026, R&D costs are expected to be around €1.2 billion, which is only 3% higher than Q2 2025, and SG&A costs are expected to be around €0.3 billion, which is nearly the same as Q2 2025. This means revenue growth will outpace the growth in expenses, and operating margins will expand further as profits grow faster than revenues.

Valuation – Historical Multiples

Since ASML has a near-monopoly in EUV lithography technology, there are no real peers to compare its valuation multiples to. Additionally, the nature of the business can be quite lumpy and cyclical, which makes a DCF challenging. Hence, the main method of valuation I intend to use is comparing ASML’s current valuation multiples – trailing and forward to their historical ranges over the 5 years.

Typically, I would take the past 10 or 15 years' data, but the reason I’m taking data only for the past 5 years is because that is when the world became more cognizant of the importance of semiconductor chips and, by extension, ASML’s monopoly and its criticality in the semiconductor supply chain.

The below table gathers data on ASML’s current valuation multiples from the company’s valuation metrics page available on Seeking Alpha. Missing data marked with an asterisk was filled using S&P Capital IQ. I calculated the % upside/downside in the stock for each valuation multiple so that it would reach its 5-year average. I then took an average across the upside/downside for all valuation multiples, which gave us an average downside of 11.57%. Based on Friday’s closing price of $1,459.80, that gives us a price target of $1,291.

Risks

Export Controls – China Revenue

In all of the last three full years, China has accounted for at least 29% of ASML’s net system sales. In Q1 2026, that figure dropped to 19%. The decline began after U.S. restrictions on exports were broadened to include more fab locations, mostly in China. As a result, ASML’s net new system sales to China declined by nearly 10% in 2025 compared to the previous year. For 2026, I have taken the estimates based on an estimate of 38 billion euros in revenues (midpoint of the company’s guidance) and a 72% share of system sales in the overall sales mix, with China contributing 19% of system sales.

My estimates may end up on the higher side, and the real numbers might be much worse. In early April 2026. A bipartisan group of lawmakers in the United States introduced the Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware [MATCH] Act, intended to cut off China from access to tools and technologies required to manufacture their own semiconductor chips, which is critical because some of them may have dual use, i.e., civilian as well as military applications.

ASML has never sold EUV lithography technology machines to China but has continued to sell DUV lithography machines, which are inferior technology compared to EUV. Some of ASML’s DUV machines are subject to Dutch export licenses issued by the USA. The MATCH Act, if passed, would prohibit ASML from selling even the DUV machines to China, putting further downward pressure on the company’s revenue and future growth.

ASML expects China to account for 20% of its total sales in 2026, meaning approximately €7.6 billion, down from 33% in 2025, or approximately €10.8 billion. So even without this act, ASML will lose €3.2 billion in sales from China, and it could get much worse. This not only impacts revenue and growth prospects this year but also future growth prospects. In my opinion, with trade/soft war involving key materials and minerals continuing between the U.S. and China, there will likely be at least a quota system or limits on ASML’s sales to China, if not a complete restriction on DUV sales. I believe the market is overlooking this risk amid a market driven by Iran headlines.

Conclusion

I want to conclude this article by placing a Hold rating on ASML stock with a price target of $1,291. ASML’s guidance assumes nearly 20% revenue growth in H2, which I feel is a bit optimistic, and there is risk to the downside. The potential restriction on DUV sales to China also creates downside risk for the stock. However, factors such as operating leverage and the criticality of the company in the ongoing strong AI build-out provide a cushion to the downside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Credo's Optical Pivot Is Changing The Entire Narrative
data.description:
Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) is no longer a story about a good quarter in the wider AI story. In earlier discussion, CRDO has outperformed the overall market and the next question is whether we have a case of good temporary demand or the initial phase in the buildout of the vertically integrated interconnect platforms. The DustPhotonics acquisition puts CRDO in a different space as it goes from being just component suppliers to becoming signal providers by virtue of having DSP, PICs, and system integration capabilities.

From Breakdown to Breakout

I believe that the fall in CRDO’s valuation to below $100 was driven by the change in narrative and not by any deterioration in quarterly results. While earnings were quite impressive, investors started pricing the scenario when the dominance of Credo in AECs would be only temporary, and the whole ecosystem of chipmakers was going to shift to silicon photonics and optical interconnects. The second part here is that momentum rotation into other stocks, such as those from Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI), Lumentum Holdings (LITE) and Coherent Corp (COHR), became obvious once the industry got confirmation regarding the role of optics in future AI scaling.

What made the selloff sharper was that CRDO’s growth, while explosive, was still concentrated as the top hyperscalers accounted for the major portion of CRDO's run-rate. Besides, operating costs surged to ~$77 million as the company ramped up its research and development activities. All things considered, CRDO stock price had already gone from perfect execution to "what if there is no smooth scalability."

The breakout above $170 is explained by a change in perception. The DustPhotonics acquisition up to ~$1.3 billion allowed the company to incorporate silicon photonics PIC technology into its product portfolio and plan to deliver $500 million+ in optical revenue by FY27. Now CRDO is present in all major components needed for a chip-based interconnect, including SerDes, DSP, AEC and photonics. Moreover, the revenue trajectory is expected to reach ~$1.3 billion+ by FY26 and grow >50% by FY27.

Why the DustPhotonics Acquisition Is About More Than Numbers

The headline terms are easy enough to grasp with $750 million in cash, about 0.92 million shares upfront and as much as 3.21 million additional shares tied to milestones. Credo anticipates the transaction's closing in the second quarter of 2026 and its non-GAAP EPS accretion in fiscal 2027.

At first glance, this seems like an example of a fast-growing company paying a hefty sum for increasing its optical presence in order to capture more business. In my opinion, there is a different interpretation: the management saw that their reliance on third parties for silicon photonics capabilities created serious restrictions to the company's future development.

What matters to me here is that DustPhotonics offers fabless silicon photonics PIC capabilities in 400G, 800G and 1.6T products, as well as a road map for further expansion to 3.2T technology. Moreover, Credo tied this asset directly to the development of ZeroFlap optical transceivers, near-port optics and co-packaged optics.

According to management, the company is counting on its ZeroFlap transceiver, optical DSP and silicon photonics solution line to generate over $500 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. Such statements after reporting on a quarter where electrical connectivity was dominant cannot be considered a diversification step.

Here comes a critical sentence: acquiring the necessary PIC capability should mitigate supply chain risk and improve cost structure going forward. This statement should not be overlooked, as there are many cases in the market where issues such as power, reliability, yields and deployment speed influence customer purchasing decisions. Therefore, being able to provide vertically integrated solutions may become the difference between being a component supplier and the core of the entire platform.

Valuation Has Become a Test for Credo's Credibility

CRDO is not an options play anymore at ~$174, its price is built around execution already having been demonstrated and maintained. Given expected revenue for fiscal year 2026 at ~$1.3-$1.35 billion this represents a forward P/S ratio of ~24x which, again, is pricey even among the sector but close to industry's leaders like Astera Labs (ALAB) and LITE.

The question isn't whether such a multiple is justified in and of itself, but rather the underlying assumptions one has to take into account here. Assuming that at this price point, the market believes in the long-term durability of current margins (approaching ~50% non-GAAP op margin), and the ability of the optical segment to ramp quickly enough to justify the ~$1.3 billion DustPhotonics investment, while reducing overall customer concentration risks at the same time.

On this basis, if we assume the company to be able to deliver >50% revenue growth going into fiscal year 2027 and optical revenue to exceed $500 million, the company's current valuation starts to make sense. However, I'd still describe it as very fragile and not forgiving. While the stock is resilient to execution headwinds, it is highly sensitive to any changes to the narrative, and will respond negatively to any signs that Credo fails to transform into an interconnect platform, specifically the optical segment.

Risks That Would Force Me to Reframe

While a certain slowdown in revenue is normal after such an incredible growth rate, the problem for the thesis will begin if the sales growth will continue despite worsening margins. In such a scenario, DustPhotonics' acquisition will not help Credo in expanding its portfolio as quickly as required and generating higher revenues from optical products. It may turn out that Credo paid for its optionality, whereas the market assumed that such development is inevitable anyway.

Secondly, customer concentration will be another problem to worry about. It is quite possible that in periods of rapid sales increase the influence of one or two big customers will become overwhelming, and it might cause Credo's future underperformance.

Other risks worth mentioning are lack of integration discipline in light of multiple activities. So, buying a small company with 70 employees that focuses on silicon photonics technology is not such a massive move, but it is a considerable strategic change for Credo at a very active moment of its development. If the company fails to execute properly and loses control over expenses, multiple investors will not forgive its current margin level.

Final Take

I am still bullish about Credo although not carelessly. From my perspective, it looks like a promising platform transition that is presented as a momentum stock. As such, the situation looks attractive but requires careful positioning. Therefore, I will remain constructive as long as optical products' sales growth begins to show positive trends.

data.title: Update On Southern Copper- The Bullish Trend Continues
data.description:
I concluded a December 23, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on copper and Southern Copper Corporation (SCCO) with the following:

Mining shares provide leverage to the underlying commodities during bull markets. However, falling copper prices could have the opposite impact on SCCO and other copper mining companies. Copper’s fundamentals and technicals are aligned and bullish as the market heads into 2026, and I expect SCCO to continue outperforming the futures and forwards as it makes higher highs. I rate SCCO a buy, even though the stock is at a record high in late December. However, buying SCCO on price weakness has been optimal over the past years, so I would leave plenty of room to add on any downside corrections.

Nearby COMEX copper futures were trading at $5.5655 per pound on December 23, 2025, with SCCO shares at just below $149. Copper and SCCO have rallied to new highs, with copper futures reaching $6.5830 per pound in late January, and SCCO shares rising to a record high of $223.88. At over $6 per pound on April 21, copper futures were considerably higher than on December 23, 2025. SCCO at over $186 on April 21 has far outperformed copper futures on a percentage basis.

Higher lows and higher highs for copper and SCCO shares

The ten-year continuous COMEX copper futures chart highlights the pattern of higher lows and higher highs since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low.

The monthly chart illustrates that the last time copper futures traded below $2 per pound was in March 2020. The pattern of higher lows and higher highs dates back over a quarter of a century. At the most recent high of $6.5830, copper futures were 233.7% higher from the March 2020 low of $1.9725 per pound.

The monthly chart of SCCO shares shows the same pattern that has taken it 855.5% higher from $23.43 to $223.88 per share.

Southern Copper shares have outperformed copper prices on a percentage basis, as the producer has delivered a leveraged return relative to the underlying commodity it extracts from Earth’s crust.

A new CEO for one of the world’s leading copper producers

Oscar Gonzalez Rocha had been the President of Southern Copper Corporation since December 1999, and SCCO’s President and Chief Executive Officer since October 2004. In December 1999, SCCO shares were trading at the $2.50 level. At the most recent high, the shares were nearly ninety times higher. On April 7, CEO Rocha passed away, leaving big shoes to fill for the company’s next leader. On April 17, the company appointed current board member Leonardo Contreras Lerdo de Tejada as interim CEO while it “conducts a search for a permanent CEO as part of its internal succession planning process.”

Lower political risk for SCCO shares after Peru reauthorizes a critical mining permit

SCCO’s CEO passed away at a time when the company received good news from the Peruvian government. After a fresh review of Southern Copper’s $1.8 billion Tia Maria copper mining project, Peru reauthorized its mining permit.

Tia Maria is expected to start production by the end of 2026 or in early 2027, with annual copper production of 120,000 metric tons over a projected 20-year lifespan.

Peru’s Ministry of Mines, “confirmed the project that has met the necessary regulatory requirements, including environmental certification, ownership, and safety and health obligations.”

The bottom line is that the reauthorization reduces some of the political risks associated with the Tia Maria mining project.

A February 25, 2026, Investing News Network article, listed Southern Copper Corporation as the fifth leading copper producer, with 883,462 tons of production, behind fourth place Glencore (GLNCY), and ahead of sixth place Anglo American.

The chart shows that SCCO ranks third by total market capitalization, behind BHP and Rio Tinto. At $184.73 per share, SCCO had a market cap of $157.58 billion. SCCO is a highly liquid stock that trades an average of over 1.395 million shares per day and pays its shareholders a $3.97 annual dividend, yielding 2.15%.

The risks rise with the prices

SCCO and copper futures have experienced incredible growth since the pandemic-inspired low in March 2020.

Copper futures rallied 233.7% from the low in 2020 to the most recent high in late January 2026. Over the same period, SCCO shares rose 855.5%, from $23.43 to $223.88 per share.

Copper and other metals prices corrected from the late January 2026 high, falling 20.3% before recovering to move above the $6 level on April 21. Over the same period, SCCO’s correction was 32.4%. While SCCO outperformed copper on the upside, the shares underperformed the red metal on the downside as the copper producer’s shares have been a leveraged copper investment vehicle.

While copper and SCCO shares remain in bullish trends, the higher copper and the shares rise, the greater the risk of sudden corrections, as we saw in late January.

Factor Grades- Caution on valuation

Seeking Alpha’s latest Factor Grades for SCCO are as follows:

SCCO receives a D grade in the valuation category due to its high price-to-earnings ratio compared to other commodity producers. Growth receives a passing C grade, while it earns A- grades in the momentum and revisions categories. SCCO’s latest earnings, reported on January 27, 2026, showed that the rally in copper prices led it to beat analysts’ forecasts for EPS and revenue. SCCO gets an A+ in the profitability category.

Seeking Alpha’s Dividend Grades for SCCO shares are in positive territory, reflecting the over 2% yield and growth history.

I have been bullish on copper and SCCO shares for a long time, and the bullish trend continues. However, I would only buy or add to long positions on significant dips as we have witnessed since late January. I rate SCCO a buy below $190 per share, leaving room to add on further declines. Critical technical support for the long-term bullish trend is at the most recent March 2026 low of $151.37 per share. Production from the Tia Maria project could go a long way in improving SCCO’s valuation issues

data.title: Ponce Financial Group's Quality Shines Through (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the last few months now, one bank that has done exceptionally well from a share price appreciation perspective has been Ponce Financial Group, Inc. (PDLB). The stock is up 16.3% since I reaffirmed the business as a Hold candidate back in the middle of November of last year. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up just 5.8%. This move higher came as a result of major improvements in revenue and profitability. The company is seeing a nice expansion in its net interest margin thanks to changing market conditions and continued shifts in the composition of its balance sheet.

After seeing the improvements that the company exhibited, I must say that I should have upgraded it sooner. The firm is well positioned for a declining interest rate environment despite the fact that most of the loans on its books are variable in nature. Management has already demonstrated meaningful improvements when it comes to both the balance sheet and the income statement. And with asset quality as high as it is, I think that upgrading it to a soft Buy is appropriate here.

Of course, that picture could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, on April 24th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. We unfortunately don't have any estimates when it comes to analysts. But given how the company has performed recently, I would wager that 2026 will start to look healthier for the company than 2025 did.

Some Stellar Results

As of the time of this writing, the newest data that investors have regarding Ponce Financial Group covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, deposits came in at $2.05 billion. This represents a nice jump over the $1.90 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This is in spite of the fact that brokered deposit exposure for the institution remains quite low. Only about 3.3% of all deposits fell under the brokered category at the end of 2025. This is also positive because it means that the company's expansion has been organic in nature. And it is also great because brokered deposits tend to be more costly than organic deposits are.

One other thing I would like to point out about the deposit situation of the institution is the fact that only $454.5 million, or 22%, of all of its deposits are uninsured. I typically like to see this number come in no higher than 30%. So on that front, Ponce Financial Group is doing quite well.

As deposits increase, the value of loans can increase also. Today, loans amount to about $2.60 billion. About a year earlier, that was $2.29 billion. The most significant exposure that the institution has here would be the $854.1 million tied up in construction and land loans. But not far behind would be the $756.5 million invested in multifamily residential properties. There are some other meaningful categories also, such as non-residential properties at $526.2 million and investor-owned assets at $307.3 million. But on the whole, the loan situation for the institution seems stable.

It would be unwise for us to ignore other parts of the balance sheet also. Take, if you will, securities. From 2023 to 2024, these dropped from $581.7 million to $472.9 million. And by the final quarter of 2025, they had dropped even more to $365.2 million. At the same time as this, cash and cash equivalents fell from $139.8 million to $126.2 million. And debt remained flat at $596.1 million. What this all indicates to me is that management has made a deliberate decision to go almost all in on loans. The goal here is certainly to improve overall profitability. And honestly, that does seem to be working out nicely.

In the chart above, you can see the net interest margin for the company for the most recent quarter compared to the same time last year, as well as for 2025 compared to 2024. There was a nice improvement year over year. This came as the yield on loans for the institution increased and as a cutting of the interest rates by the Federal Reserve allowed the firm to pay not only debtholders less, but depositors less also. The fact that securities are yielding less is evidence to me that management made a wise decision to shift some of their assets from securities to loans.

In the chart above, you can see the end result of the growth of the company's balance sheet and the changes in its composition combined with improvements in the interest rate environment. Net interest income increased, working to push net profits up nicely. The $9.9 million in net income for the final quarter of 2025 translates to an annualized rate of profitability of $39.6 million. In the chart below, you can see what this means for the business on a price-to-earnings basis. If instead of using the results from 2025, we use projected figures for 2026, you would get a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.7. That is, unfortunately, still a bit above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. However, only three of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart are cheaper than it is. And one of those is just barely cheaper. So compared to other similar firms, I would say it is comparable in this regard.

In the chart below, you can see a very similar type of analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, the institution is actually near the more expensive end of the spectrum. Four of the five institutions I stacked it up against are cheaper than it is. But on a price-to-tangible book basis, only two of them ended up being cheaper. When you average all of this out, you do end up with a firm that is, once again, around the middle of the group.

This is a common theme when it comes to Ponce Financial Group. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading for the business of 1.26%. That is actually above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see. But with two of the five businesses that I compared it to having a reading higher than what it does, it is once again near the middle of the group. The subsequent chart below that shows the same type of analysis using return on equity. Once again, two of the firms are superior to it, even though the 12.50% reading that we get is comfortably above the 10% minimum that I like to see.

Probably my only issue with the business would be its credit quality metrics. Unfortunately, 1.02% of its loans and 0.83% of its assets were classified as non-performing at the end of the most recent quarter. These numbers are not only above the 0.87% and 0.69%, respectively, that the business saw a year earlier. They also happen to be well above the 0.50% maximum that I like to see. So this certainly needs to be monitored moving forward.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. That should occur on April 24th. Unfortunately, analysts have not provided any estimates for the business. But for context, in the first quarter of 2025, revenue was $24.6 million. Earnings per share amounted to $0.25, which translated to net profits of $5.7 million. Considering how the company has performed as of late, I wouldn't be surprised if the numbers this year are higher.

The last thing I would like to touch on is that, for those looking for a declining interest rate environment, Ponce Financial Group is certainly worth considering. In the image below, you can see what would happen to net interest income over the course of a year if the business were to be hit by a 1% drop in interest rates. In this case, we would see a bit of an increase. In fact, up until a 300 basis point decline in interest rates, you would actually see net interest income rise. This is intriguing when you consider that, for all loans that come due more than one year out from the most recent quarter, only 36.6% have fixed rates. But because of the changes that the business would see in the cost of deposits and debt, the decline associated with adjustable-rate loans is more than offset.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my sincere belief that I should have upgraded Ponce Financial Group previously. I'm not just saying that because of the missed upside. Rather, it has to do with the fact that management has done a solid job as of late. Moving forward, I expect that the company will benefit from declines in the interest rate environment, even if that is short term. And when you look at asset quality and other factors such as growth, I think that upgrading it to a soft Buy is warranted.

data.title: Madison Air Solutions: Good Growth, But Overvalued Stock
data.description:
Madison Air Solutions Corporation (MAIR) listed its stock on the NYSE on the 16th of April. The IPO has turned out to be successful; following the listing, Madison Air stock trades at $33.7 as of writing, well above the company’s $27 price for the stock offering. Fast growth in data center cooling solutions is driving optimism for the investment, and seemingly for good reason.

The indoor air quality company has managed to grow well during 2025, boosted by AI data center investments alongside other revenue streams' growth. Madison Air’s financial performance will likely remain good, but ultimately, the stock already prices in very ambitious expectations. I believe that investors are likely better off waiting for a better entry point.

An Introduction to Madison Air & Its Financials

Headquartered in Chicago, Madison Air manufactures and sells indoor air quality products. The company’s brands include Nortek Air Solutions, Big Ass Fans, Reznor, AprilAire, and a number of other names, through which Madison Air sells products such as heating, air filtration and purification, humidification, air conditioning solutions, and other products. Around two thirds of Madison Air’s revenues are from commercial customers, and the remainder is from residential customers.

Madison Air estimates that the company holds around an 8% market share of a large $40 billion TAM in North America; Madison Air’s market position is noteworthy alongside AAON, Inc. (AAON), Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT), Trane Technologies plc (TT), and other key competitors. The company has acquired a total of 13 businesses since 2017 to grow its market potential. M&A activity is a core factor in Madison Air’s strategy.

Madison Air’s recent financial performance has been very attractive. The company generated $3.3 billion in revenues in 2025, growing by 27.3% year-on-year from 2024. Underlying organic revenue growth has been approximately 7.8% from 2020 to 2025. Good revenue growth has been coupled with high margins; Madison Air reported a very good 16.8% operating margin in 2025. The company requires minimal CapEx to grow, making Madison Air’s free cash flow strong.

Madison Air does have quite a high amount of leverage from debt; the company’s total interest-bearing debts stand at $5.6 billion after 2025. Proceeds from the company’s IPO offering should help lower leverage, though, in my opinion making the company’s balance sheet sustainable.

The Focus Point Is on Madison Air's Data Center Growth

Madison Air should have good earnings growth ahead as well. The company manufactures cooling solutions for data centers through Nortek Data Center Cooling, creating a clear platform for growth – AI data center investments are projected to nearly triple from $600 billion in 2026 to $1.7 trillion by 2030 already according to Dell’Oro. Competitor AAON has clearly captured growth from the data center market as I’ve written in previous articles, and Madison Air is positioned to grow similarly. Madison Air enters 2026 with over $2 billion in the backlog, up by 108.6% year-on-year.

Ultimately, it’s still quite unclear how significant the data center cooling market is as a share of Madison Air’s current revenues, and how far the revenue stream can grow. Madison Air hasn’t laid out any specific financial targets yet as the IPO is very recent; extrapolating significant growth is a dangerous game. I believe that data center growth, and growth in other offerings, does create quite a good platform to grow on.

MAIR Stock Is Too Expensive; I Suggest Caution

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Madison Air to estimate a fair valuation for the stock. Due to quite limited investor relation communications so far, the model’s estimates are quite challenging to make.

As a base scenario, I expect revenue momentum to remain good organically as data center revenues grow. I estimate a 2025-2035 CAGR of 9.6% with 3% perpetual growth after 2035.

I expect margins to rise slightly over time through general operating leverage; I estimate the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 17.5%, up by 70 basis points from 16.8% in 2025. Madison Air’s minimal CapEx and non-cash amortization should make the company’s cash flow conversion from EBIT very good going forward as well.

The estimates put Madison’s fair value at $24.2, suggesting -28% downside as of writing. I believe that the stock’s post-IPO valuation is too expensive; despite ambitious cash flow growth expectations, Madison Air doesn’t seem to be worth its current price. Investors are likely better off waiting for a cheaper entry point. The company was willing to offer shares at $27 in the IPO offering, making $33.7 a not very appealing entry level.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.79%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.22% interest rate using Madison’s 2025 debt and interest expenses. I assume a fair 20% long-term debt-to-equity ratio; Madison Air’s IPO offering should help moderate leverage.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.26% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I use the engineering & construction industry’s beta estimate of 1.21 for Madison Air. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.62% and the WACC at 8.79%.

Takeaway

Madison Air seems like an exciting company following the company’s IPO offering. The company has grown well as data center cooling solutions are taking off, and other revenue streams perform well as well. Ultimately, with a limited history and no publicly available financial targets, Madison Air’s future growth rate is uncertain, but the stock already prices in very ambitious growth. Due to an unattractive valuation, I initiate MAIR stock at a Sell rating.

data.title: Red Cat: A Multi-Domain ISR Drone Winner In The Making
data.description:
During the war in Iran, we saw that neighboring countries were attacked with Shahed drones and ballistic missiles. It once again re-emphasized the need for missile defense and cost-efficient counter-drone solutions. Much to my surprise, it was drone stock companies that had limited to no counter-drone capabilities that saw their share prices surge. One of those names with lower scalable counter-drone capability is Red Cat (RCAT). In this report, I take a deeper look at the company’s investment case.

Red Cat Envisions Multi-Domain Unmanned Capability

Red Cat operates with three subsidiaries, namely Teal Drones, Flightwave, and Blue Ops. The company has the Black Widow Short Range Reconnaissance drone, for which it doubled production capacity in 2025. It is a lightweight drone equipped with Electro Optic and Infrared sensors for short-range reconnaissance missions, which would be useful for monitoring, for instance, energy infrastructure and military bases. The drone features on-board AI processing capabilities. The company also has the Edge 130 Blue, which has a 2-hour endurance when operated in fixed-wing mode, providing longer-range surveillance capability.

Blue Ops is where the company focused on multi-domain unmanned platforms. Their Variant 7 is an unmanned surface vessel expanding unmanned capabilities to the sea domain, but ideally Blue Ops is where the company also develops land and submersible unmanned and autonomous solutions. Furthermore,

For now, Red Cat’s main appeal seems to be in drone-powered ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance), but it also has a broader appeal as the company intends its multi-domain products to integrate into a single ISR platform, allowing for data acquired from multiple domains to be visualized together to get a more complete intelligence picture. The company has some counter-drone capabilities in the form of the ACS Bull Frog and the Zeus. The ACS Bull is an autonomous weapon station that can be mounted on the Variant 7. The Zeus is guided ammunition that can also be mounted on the Variant 7. This is basically counter-drone capability from other manufacturers that Red Cat can mount on its own vessel. Where Red Cat attempts to differentiate itself is by providing a unified controller that can be used for all of its products and across multiple domains to control the drones.

Red Cat Is In The Scaling Phase With Gross Margins Flipping Sign

If we look at the company’s financial performance, we see that revenues rose substantially in 2025. We do note that 2024 was a transition period of eight months as the company changed its fiscal year to end in December rather than April. So, that drives some of the increase in revenues, but in 2025, the revenues also increased as the company scaled drone deliveries under the Short Range Reconnaissance program. Gross profit increased by $3.3 million to $1.3 million, indicating that margins swung from -27.4% to 3.2%. SG&A increased due to higher stock-based compensation and labor costs supporting growing production. R&D grew 81% to $17.9 million, and other expenses were up, driven by fair value adjustments on the convertible notes and a non-recurring gain on sale in 2024, bringing the net operating loss to $66.6 million, indicating a 72.5% increase in losses.

What we see clearly is that Red Cat is in the scaling phase with gross margins turning positive, and from there, operating leverage should drive the margins further. A watch item will be the development of operating expenses.

Red Cat Gets A Speculative "Buy" Rating

Typically, I value stocks according to an EV/EBITDA method. However, since Red Cat is not generating positive EBITDA, I am using an EV/Sales method instead. That already makes Red Cat higher risk. At a peer group multiple, we see no upside. However, when applying a trading multiple that is twice that of the peer group, we see around 21% upside. So, I have a speculative "Buy" rating with a $15.24 price target, indicating around 21% upside. What should be noted is that the company will have elevated free cash flow burns this year and next year, and even in 2028, the free cash flow is not expected to turn positive. By that time, the cash position of $167.9 million will also have significantly declined. So, this is a speculative buy but with dilution risk.

Conclusion: Red Cat Is An Autonomous ISR "Buy"

When I saw shares of Red Cat increasing following the drone strikes in Iran, Red Cat was among the companies that showed a positive share price increase. However, the company does not really have a counter-drone solution that is purpose-built. It can leverage anti-drone systems built by other manufacturers, and its first-person view drone could be repurposed for anti-drone operations, but since it is not autonomous, I do not believe it is the most scalable solution. I believe that companies such as EOS have better solutions. However, I do believe that eventually this could be a strong ISR multi-domain provider, and therefore I am marking the stock a speculative "Buy."

data.title: U.S. Crude Remains Under Pressure As Bulls Stay Cautious
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

​The oil market is primarily trading as a geopolitical asset rather than a classic supply-demand balance instrument. Any news surrounding the U.S. and Iran, as well as the Strait of Hormuz, triggers sharp upside moves on supply disruption risks and equally rapid pullbacks on de-escalation.

The main source of volatility is the risk of a supply shock in the Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical chokepoint for global energy, so even localized deterioration is immediately priced in via a risk premium. This makes the market nervous and thin, with speculative positioning amplifying swings.

Over a longer horizon, price pressure could intensify from rising supply. The U.S. continues ramping up production, with EIA pointing to record levels in 2026, limiting the sustainability of any price rally. A broader market tilt toward oversupply would further cap upside if the geopolitical premium fades quickly.

Global demand remains resilient, with the summer season traditionally supporting fuel consumption. However, at elevated price levels, the risk of demand destruction grows, particularly if the geopolitical premium persists longer than anticipated. Thus, current price support appears more reactive than sustainably bullish.

Amid expectations of U.S.-Iran talks, WTI (CL1:COM) quotes continue facing selling pressure, capping upside attempts at resistance around $87.60-88.40 per barrel. Support at $85.40-85.20 is holding for now, but risks of a breakdown toward $83.00-82.00 remain.

Nevertheless, Middle East escalation risks stay elevated, so bulls should remain extremely cautious.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
